messages.content: random thing i've always wondered, why do we get hiccups? like what's the actual point of them

The honest answer is that nobody's entirely sure — hiccups seem to be one of those evolutionary leftovers without a clear modern purpose.

The leading theory is that it's a reflex inherited from our fish-like ancestors, where a similar mechanism helped push water over gills. In us, it just fires occasionally when the diaphragm gets irritated — eating too fast, a cold drink, a sudden surprise.

What made you think of that today?
my niece had them forever yesterday and it got me curious about the whole thing

That tracks — there's something about watching someone else hiccup endlessly that makes you really notice how strange the whole thing is.

Kids seem to get them more dramatically somehow. How old is your niece?
i swear everyone's got their own folk cure too. my grandma always did the spoonful of sugar thing

The sugar one is actually one of the more plausible folk remedies — the idea is that swallowing something unexpected might reset the vagus nerve and interrupt the reflex. Whether it works or it's just distraction is hard to say.

Do you have a go-to cure you swear by, or are you a skeptic?
it's kinda funny how something so common is apparently still a bit of a mystery science-wise

It really is — and hiccups aren't alone in that. There are a handful of things the body does constantly that we still can't fully explain, like why we yawn or why we dream.

It's a good reminder that "common" and "understood" aren't the same thing.
